did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the YOLOv10x deep learning object detection model to detect and classify skin lesions (including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) from dermoscopic images in the HAM10000 dataset. The study involved training the model, performing data preprocessing (oversampling, augmentation), and evaluating performance metrics (precision, recall, mAP50, F1-score) to obtain results on skin disease detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training and evaluation of the YOLOv10x deep learning model on the HAM10000 dataset for skin disease detection. It includes specific sections on Materials and Methods (dataset preprocessing, model training parameters), Results (performance metrics like precision, recall, mAP50, and F1-score), and Discussion. Although published in a conference proceedings (SIML), it reports new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: YOLOv10x Model for Accurate First Detection of Skin Diseases from Dermoscopic Objects
journal: 2025 International Conference on Smart Computing, IoT and Machine Learning (SIML)
brief_summary: This study evaluates the YOLOv10x deep learning model for the early detection and classification of seven skin lesion types using the HAM10000 dermoscopic dataset. To address class imbalance, the researchers applied undersampling, oversampling, and data augmentation techniques to create a balanced training set of 3,500 images. The model was trained on a Kaggle platform with dual T4 GPUs and evaluated using precision, recall, mAP50, and F1-score metrics. The YOLOv10x model achieved a precision of 84.2%, recall of 86.3%, mAP50 of 89.2%, and an F1-score of 85.2%. Testing on a subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset further validated the model's ability to accurately discern malignant and benign lesions. The results demonstrate the potential of YOLOv10x as a robust, real-time diagnostic tool for improving skin cancer detection accuracy.

all_task_types.target: Bounding boxes and class labels for skin lesions in dermoscopic images.
all_task_types.details: The paper uses the YOLOv10x object detection model to identify and localize skin lesions. The model predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities for seven distinct lesion types (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Vascular Lesions) from the HAM10000 dataset. The task is explicitly framed as object detection, which involves simultaneous prediction of bounding boxes and class labels, evaluated using metrics like mAP50, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset sourced from the ISIC collection, comprising 10,015 images with accompanying annotations. The images were obtained through various procedures from diverse populations and are openly accessible for research in dermoscopic image recognition.

use.training: Used for training the YOLOv10x model. The original imbalanced dataset was preprocessed using undersampling (majority class reduced) and oversampling/augmentation (minority class expanded) to balance each class to 500 images, resulting in a balanced training set of 3,500 images. This balanced subset was split into 80% for training and 10% for validation.
use.validation: Used for validation. 10% of the balanced dataset (350 images) was used for validation.
use.testing: Used for testing. 10% of the balanced dataset (350 images) was used for testing.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A publicly available benchmark dataset in dermatology research containing high-resolution dermoscopic images of various skin lesion types. A subset was used for external testing to evaluate the model's generalization.

use.testing: Used for external testing to evaluate the model's ability to discern malignant and benign lesions and generalize across multiple skin cancer types. A balanced selection of 10 images per class was used.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (precision, recall, mAP50, F1-score) and a confusion matrix on a test subset.

HAM10000.description: Training and validation results on the preprocessed, balanced HAM10000 dataset (3,500 images: 80% train, 10% val, 10% test). The model was trained for 100 epochs on Kaggle with dual T4 GPUs.
HAM10000.notes: The paper reports these aggregate metrics for the model trained on the balanced HAM10000 subset. No per-class breakdown is provided for this specific training set in the text, though a confusion matrix (Fig. 5) is referenced.

ISIC_2019.description: External testing results on a balanced subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset (70 images total, 10 images per class) to evaluate generalization.
ISIC_2019.notes: Detection rates are reported as counts of correctly identified images out of 10 per class. Lower detection rates for DF and BKL were attributed to subtle visual similarities in shape, color, and contrast with other lesion types.

key_features: Dual assignment strategy (integrating one-to-many and one-to-one label assignments)
architecture_details.reason_for_selection: Exceptional performance, precision and accuracy-focused design for complex detection tasks

training_details.dataset: Preprocessed and balanced HAM10000 dataset (3,500 images)

classification_or_output_head.output: Bounding boxes and class probabilities for 7 lesion types

preprocessing_techniques: Undersampling of the majority class to mitigate class imbalance
Oversampling of the minority class to mitigate class imbalance
Data augmentation techniques including rotation (90° clockwise and counterclockwise), flipping (vertical and horizontal), zooming, and brightness adjustments
Annotation of images using Roboflow software

conclusions: The YOLOv10x model achieved high detection accuracy on the balanced HAM10000 dataset, with a precision of 84.2%, recall of 86.3%, mAP50 of 89.2%, and an F1-score of 85.2%.

The model demonstrated strong generalization capabilities on an external ISIC 2019 test subset, successfully detecting all images for Melanoma (MEL), Melanocytic Nevi (NV), and Vascular Lesions (VASC).
Detection performance was slightly lower for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Actinic Keratosis (AKIEC), with 9 out of 10 images correctly identified in the external test set.

The lowest detection rates were observed for Dermatofibroma (DF) and Benign Keratosis-like Lesions (BKL), with 8 out of 10 images correctly identified, attributed to subtle visual similarities in shape, color, and contrast with other lesion types.
The study underscores the potential of YOLOv10x as a robust, real-time diagnostic tool for improving skin cancer detection accuracy and reducing reliance on expert interpretation.

Future work should focus on refining the model with larger and more diverse datasets, incorporating expert-labelled annotations, and exploring architectural modifications to improve feature extraction and classification for challenging classes.
important_remarks: The study relies on a heavily manipulated subset of the HAM10000 dataset (3,500 images) created by undersampling the majority class and oversampling/augmenting minority classes to achieve perfect class balance (500 images per class), which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence.

External validation was performed on a very small, artificially balanced subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset (only 10 images per class, totaling 70 images), limiting the statistical power and generalizability of the generalization results.
The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, relying solely on point estimates.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing or significance analysis was conducted to compare the model's performance against baseline methods or to validate the observed differences between classes.

Performance on Dermatofibroma (DF) and Benign Keratosis-like Lesions (BKL) was lower (8/10 detection rate) due to subtle visual similarities with other classes, indicating potential limitations in distinguishing fine-grained benign lesions.
The model was trained on Kaggle using dual T4 GPUs, but specific hyperparameters such as learning rate, batch size, and optimizer settings are not reported, limiting reproducibility.
The input image resolution was reduced to 224x224 pixels, which may result in the loss of fine-grained dermoscopic features critical for accurate diagnosis of small or subtle lesions.
The study is published in a conference proceedings (SIML) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.
